summary: DHH argues that the failed campaign to cancel Joe Rogan over COVID misinformation on Spotify represents the peak of cancel culture. Despite weeks of media outrage, open letters, and a trending #CancelSpotify hashtag, Spotify grew to 182 million subscribers and Rogan gained 2 million new listeners — a textbook Streisand effect. DHH contends that when the public examined the actual evidence, the charges seemed overblown, which permanently damaged the credibility of those making them. He predicts this dynamic is already carrying over to the media reaction against Elon Musk's Twitter acquisition.
key_points: Spotify's Q1 numbers showed unimpeded growth despite the cancel campaign, proving the listening public rejected the outrage
Overblown charges against a popular figure backfire via the Streisand effect, indicting the accusers rather than the accused
Each failed cancel attempt increases the public's 'discount factor' on future moral outrage from the same voices
The cycle of outrage moves so fast that each crisis feels ancient within months, undermining its own seriousness
The Rogan episode's failure is already affecting public reception of similar outrage directed at Elon Musk
money_quotes: The listening public has spoken about the grave dangers posed by the man with the microphone: It doesn't give a shit.

That's the problem with bringing out the big guns. When you trump up charges of crimes against humanity against a mild-mannered, broadly intellectually curious, and all-round popular podcast host, it's a big gamble!

When folks then examine the evidence for themselves, and actually listen to an episode of Rogan, and they fail to find even the most basic substantiation of the charges? Well, it naturally indicts the indicter rather than the indictee.
It also leaves a permanent mark of 'wait, we were freaking the fuck out over this???' in the public psyche.
When the same segment invokes the same moral outrage in the same shrill tones, the discount factor applied to the message is rapidly increasing.
The Current Thing is at once both the most important topic in the world and a memory hole to be forgotten five minutes later.

You always have to be careful not to tip so far that the mere presence of the shrill voices mean you take the other side out of reflex, but it's fair to say that you can easily turn the volume on them down.
key_insight: When cancel campaigns massively overstate their case against popular figures, the public backlash permanently erodes the credibility of future outrage from the same voices.
